what-the-terminal-is.label: What the Terminal Is
what-the-terminal-is.summary: Type simple commands and read the terminal response.

sketch0.title: The terminal is a conversation with the computer

sketch1.title: The prompt waits for your next command

sc-terminal-purpose.title: What is the terminal mainly for?
sc-terminal-purpose.prompt: It lets you type commands for the computer to run.
sc-terminal-purpose.hint: It lets you type commands for the computer to run.

help.concept: It lets you type commands for the computer to run.
help.hint_1: It lets you type commands for the computer to run.
help.hint_2: Review the command names from the lesson, then choose the option that matches the task.

options.a: A text-based place to run commands
options.b: Only a place to draw pictures

mc-safe-first.title: Choose all safe beginner commands from this lesson.
mc-safe-first.prompt: Look for commands that ask simple questions without changing files.
mc-safe-first.hint: Choose commands that inspect the session or workspace.

help.concept: Early terminal practice should start with commands that show information.
help.hint_1: Choose commands that inspect the session or workspace.
help.hint_2: Review the command names from the lesson, then choose the option that matches the task.

ci-create-linux-start.title: Run your first location check
ci-create-linux-start.prompt: Run `pwd` to ask the terminal where this workspace starts. Check your answer after you see `/workspace`.
ci-create-linux-start.hint: Type `pwd`, press Enter, then check the terminal output.

help.concept: The terminal can answer simple questions before you change any files.
help.hint_1: Type `pwd`, press Enter, then check the terminal output.

ci-make-command-practice.title: Check the session user
ci-make-command-practice.prompt: Run `whoami` to see the Linux user name for this terminal session.
ci-make-command-practice.hint: Type `whoami`, press Enter, and check the response.

help.concept: `whoami` prints the user account running the terminal session.
help.hint_1: Type `whoami`, press Enter, and check the response.
help.hint_2: Submit after the command appears in the terminal history.

ci-create-first-lab.title: List the starting workspace
ci-create-first-lab.prompt: Run `ls` to see what is already in the workspace. You should see `README.md`.
ci-create-first-lab.hint: Type `ls`, press Enter, and look for `README.md` in the output.

help.concept: `ls` lists the files and folders in your current workspace.
help.hint_1: Type `ls`, press Enter, and look for `README.md` in the output.
help.hint_2: Submit after `README.md` appears in the terminal output.

try-terminal-see-workspace.title: Ask where you are
try-terminal-see-workspace.prompt: Run `pwd` and look for the workspace path.
try-terminal-see-workspace.hint: Type `pwd`, press Enter, then check for `/workspace`.

help.concept: `pwd` prints the folder where the terminal is currently working.
help.hint_1: Type `pwd`, press Enter, then check for `/workspace`.
help.hint_2: Run the command in the terminal, check the response, then submit.

try-terminal-print-user.title: Print the terminal user
try-terminal-print-user.prompt: Run `whoami` to see the user for this terminal session.

help.hint_2: Run the command in the terminal, check the response, then submit.

try-terminal-list-start.title: List the starting files
try-terminal-list-start.hint: Type `ls`, press Enter, and look for `README.md`.

help.concept: `ls` lists the files and folders in the current folder.
help.hint_1: Type `ls`, press Enter, and look for `README.md`.
help.hint_2: Run the command in the terminal, check the response, then submit.

terminal-is-conversation.title: The terminal listens for one command at a time
terminal-is-conversation.bodyMarkdown:
A terminal is a direct way to ask Linux to do work. You type a command, press Enter, and read the response.

Start with commands that answer simple questions:

```bash
whoami
pwd
ls
```

These commands are safe first steps because they show information without changing the workspace.

prompt-and-command.title: The prompt is your starting line
prompt-and-command.bodyMarkdown:
The prompt marks where the next command goes. Type one command, press Enter, then read the response before typing the next one.

Safe first commands:

```bash
pwd
whoami
ls
```

Use `ls` often. It tells you what is in the current folder.

safe-first-habits.title: Small commands are easier to trust
safe-first-habits.bodyMarkdown:
Good terminal habits start with checking before changing. Look around with `pwd` and `ls`, then decide what to do next.

For now, stay inside the lesson workspace and avoid delete commands. You will practice file changes later with small files that are safe to throw away.
